old_claim.note: Hospital Information Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from laparoscopic_surgery to hospital_information_systems; laparoscopic surgery depends on operative visualization and surgical practice, not hospital IT.

source_shape.source_locator: JSLS history article on the first laparoscopic cholecystectomy and its instruments.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The article explains early laparoscopic cholecystectomy through the laparoscope, hemoclip, scissors, endoscopic technology, and surgical training, without requiring hospital information systems.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes HIS as a direct prerequisite because the surgical method is defined by instruments, visualization, and operative technique rather than record or workflow software.

ontology_before: Laparoscopic surgery depended on hospital information systems, mixing operative technique with later administrative infrastructure.
ontology_after: Laparoscopic surgery is modeled through endoscopic visualization and aseptic surgical practice.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: hospital information systems no longer appear as a direct laparoscopic-surgery prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were re-scoped to operating-room scheduling or surgical documentation software.
A source showed laparoscopic operations require hospital information-system infrastructure as part of the method.
Removing HIS also removed the endoscopic visualization dependency.
short_reason: Hospital information systems are not a laparoscopic-surgery method prerequisite.
